insight: Absorb denial strategies include: syncing Iso Kronus against big defenders when lacking other attack, spamming Thermites to delay opponent's absorb access, or 'gambiting' defense by allowing a breach to force opponent resource expenditure.
author: spiritfryer
context: Discussion of defensive strategies and how to interact with absorb mechanics in Prismata.

insight: Prismata gameplay is less about committing to pre-planned strategies and more about identifying which unit combination is strongest in a given set; some sets may have multiple viable combos, but players don't queue ranked with a fixed strat in mind.
author: spiritfryer
context: Discussion of strategy vs set-reading in competitive Prismata.

insight: Venge Cannon is unique in that it can make some sets breachproof-viable on its own without any other breachproof units.
author: spiritfryer
context: Discussion of breachproof strategies and Venge Cannon's role.

insight: In some sets, optimal play involves neither player committing to a specific strategy early, instead delaying commitment until a key turning point where one player's commitment would be countered; early commitment can lead to being punished.
author: spiritfryer, awaclus
context: Discussion of whether players can force opponents to lose through deceptive strategy.

insight: When opponent goes breachproof, value attack slightly less since breachproof's advantage is unit health efficiency over soak; before opponent commits to breachproof, prioritize attack value to force them into earlier Forcefield purchases or delaying breachproof transition.
author: jamberine
context: Tactical adjustments when facing breachproof strategies.

insight: In breachproof matchups, economy units are typically less breachproof-efficient than attacker units; hit economy units when supply is low or defense is needed, otherwise prioritize attackers.
author: jamberine
context: Targeting priorities when attacking breachproof opponents.

insight: Gauss Cannon is primarily effective in breachproof strategies; some players argue its supply should be increased to make breachproof more viable, while others contend breachproof is appropriately balanced by being rarely viable.
author: pikachumemes, awaclus
context: Debate over breachproof viability and balance of Gauss Cannon supply.

insight: Animus's lack of granularity can force red waste, but it remains a good deal if the game is fast enough that building additional drones to spend wasted red is impractical; efficient attackers (like Shadowfang) can be built despite one red waste.
author: velizar_
context: Discussion of Animus efficiency and resource waste in fast games.

insight: Buying tech buildings for specific legendary units and then running out of that unit's supply forces inefficient spending of the tech; conversely, sometimes overtecking just to access a legendary unit is justified.
author: awaclus
context: Discussion of overextension and inefficient tech spending around legendary units.

insight: Gauss Cannon is good in breachproof but generally shouldn't be a main attack force; it's worthwhile when it will deal damage and player would otherwise float excess gold/green, but Xaetron or other units typically provide better value.
author: zera777
context: Evaluation of Gauss Cannon value in specific game situations.

insight: As P2, DD/DA and DD/DDC are standard turn 2 plays (often superior to DD/DD which floats resources); DD/DD wins less than 5% of sets and is generally poor for P2.
author: pikachumemes, awaclus
context: Analysis of P2 turn 2 opening theory and resource efficiency.

insight: As P1, DD/DD is acceptable in non-high-econ games and differs from P2's context; floating resources on P1 is more recoverable than on P2.
author: awaclus
context: Clarification on P1 vs P2 opening differences.

insight: Assigning damage to unit types impacts future board state; assigning to Engineers instead of less-valuable units can enable future purchases (e.g., assigning to Engineer over Rhino prevents breaching unit death but preserves Rhino for later attack synergies).
author: awaclus
context: Discussion of optimal damage assignment in specific game state.

insight: Hannibul is valuable in sets with Bloodragers because it blocks threat while still dealing damage; its 7 damage soak helps handle large Bloodrager-based attacks in late game.
author: awaclus
context: Unit synergy analysis in specific game with Bloodrager threats.

insight: Zemora Voidbringer is generally not good into absorb denial strategies like Bloodrager, though rare exceptions exist; Bloodrager itself is not the most convenient long-term absorb denial but can limit Zemora viability.
author: pikachumemes, awaclus
context: Discussion of Zemora viability against Bloodrager-based absorb denial.

insight: Buying drones while floating tech buildings (when already able to efficiently spend tech) is inefficient; excess tech should be spent first unless there's solid reason to prioritize drone over tech efficiency.
author: awaclus
context: Discussion of resource allocation and tech efficiency in game analysis.

insight: Buying a second Animus is generally only justified if there exists a unit costing 3+ red or a unit with red-costed click ability, except in rare very-high-econ situations.
author: awaclus
context: Analysis of multiple Animus purchases and their justification.

insight: P1 DB line is strong due to trivial Wall access in aggressive games; it can be beaten with high-econ builds or other strategies if the units are powerful enough to avoid needing Wall for extended periods.
author: awaclus
context: Analysis of P1 DB Flameanimus line and counterplay.

insight: Flameanimus with wasted red is inefficient; Conduit or Blastforge for fast Bombardiers is often better than second Flameanimus when unable to spend all generated red.
author: pikachumemes
context: Evaluation of second Flameanimus vs alternative tech in specific replay.

insight: In Thorium sets, Drone is generally more efficient than Conduit for economy when green can be spent as green; Conduit loses 33% efficiency if green floats, though Conduit provides HP value and synergizes with Drone sacrifice for Forcefield access.
author: pikachumemes, awaclus
context: Mathematical analysis of Drone vs Conduit efficiency in Thorium economy.

insight: Cauterizer as permanent attack is slightly less efficient than building it a turn later (Tantalum Ray), but becomes more efficient if opponent's Engis provide protection from threats or if blue would otherwise be wasted.
author: awaclus
context: Cost-benefit analysis of early Cauterizer purchase.

insight: Cauterizer provides granular defense and strong threat response, making it preferable to losing Rhinos against large incoming damage.
author: steel0229e
context: Situational value of Cauterizer in defensive scenarios.

insight: Clicking Smorchling in vore/smorc sets reduces inflation and eliminates need for additional red consumption; this allows delaying blue purchases or buying blue purely for Wall and Pixie.
author: steel0229e
context: Discussion of Smorchling click strategy in vore/smorc sets.

insight: Crazimus (Animus) is strengthened by units like Smorchling and Perforator, enabling aggressive early builds that threaten quick Rhino or Vore access.
author: silentslayers
context: Discussion of Crazimus synergies in vore/smorc sets.

insight: P2 buying DD/DD as opening (turn 2) is a strong early commitment that can be punished; Tatsu requires opponent to play greedily without punishing early aggression to remain viable.
author: awaclus, velizar_
context: Analysis of Tatsu viability in different set contexts.

insight: Mobile Animus is not efficient for economy compared to natural Animus or Drone economy; it is primarily justified when red granularity is critical to executing a strategy.
author: awaclus, silentslayers
context: Evaluation of Mobile Animus economy efficiency.

insight: Natural Animus is slow compared to DD/DDE; it may enable turn 4 Rhino and Phage build but requires red waste if a second Rhino or Phage is desired, making DD/DDE preferable in many cases.
author: awaclus
context: Comparison of Natural Animus vs DD/DDE economy.

insight: Early-game Tarsiers are valuable and worth more than one red per turn during their purchase; red is technically cheaper than gold (3g makes 1r vs 1e makes 1g) but early Tarsier efficiency matters more than granular cost analysis.
author: silentslayers
context: Discussion of Tarsier value vs red economy in early game.
